[179:42]when the war started, it looked like it was very one sided, very asymmetrical. And I said in the

[179:55]in that opening weekend in those first, maybe three or four days. But again,

[180:01]understanding the conflict means you have to understand the weapon systems that are being used,

[180:07]the tactics, the strategic objectives, the war only works for America if it's quick.

[180:15]We have better weapons, but we have fewer of them. And it's not as easy to make them. America's

[180:22]not an industrial power anymore. We don't make the weapons. We don't make enough of them.

[180:27]Our adversaries are the complete opposite. They make tons of weapons. And their weapons are not

[180:32]precise. They're dumb. You know, our weapons are smart. They're AI. They're connected to satellites.

[180:38]They're very sophisticated, but they're very expensive. And we don't make enough of them.

[180:43]Russia, on the other hand, makes four million drones a year, four million drones every year.

[180:50]And it's native. Iran makes hundreds of thousands of drones. And they actually design them too.

[180:57]We stole their drone design. They're Shahid Suicide drone. We just copied that model,

[181:03]reverse engineered it and made one of our own. And that's actually what we're using in this conflict,

[181:08]although we just have far fewer of them. We're looking to buy drone interceptors or even drones

[181:14]themselves from Ukraine. Again, we don't make them. So this is fundamentally the nature of modern

[181:21]conflict in the era of the Ukraine war. Now in the era of the Iran war, you have these big

[181:28]advantages that the great powers think that they have like Russia going into Ukraine. Obviously

[181:34]has a man-powered advantage. Has more equipment, has more money, has a bigger industrial base.

[181:41]And they go in hard and fast thinking they're going to topple the government and achieve their

[181:46]objectives. There's actually some striking similarities there. And what they then find is that

[181:52]they are ground down by the enemy's defensive capabilities. And it's sort of inverted.

[181:59]In Ukraine, America was supplying all of these defensive weapons for Ukraine and defensive

[182:04]fortifications and intelligence and the star shield satellite system. In Iran, it's the reverse.

[182:13]We are now encountering offensive capabilities actually, which is ballistic missiles, which are

[182:19]very cheap drones, which are very cheap, very cheap mines, unmanned naval vessels. And these are

[182:28]creating a porcupine-like effect and making it extremely complicated to fight the war.

[182:32]So we came in as the vastly superior power, again, with more weapons, more sophisticated

[182:39]weapons, much larger country thinking that all we had to do was hit Iran hard and then they would

[182:46]fall over. If that doesn't happen, you're in trouble. And that is how the war, as you know, has been

[182:52]playing out. Trump and Israel in concert hit Iran with everything they had. We bond them over 1200

[183:37]They got hit with everything the US and Israel have. But now we're entering the third week tomorrow

[183:43]night. And again, those core strategic objectives of the United States have not been achieved.

[183:50]The regime has not collapsed, has not toppled. And there is no sign that it's going to anytime soon.

[183:57]How did we actually arrive here at the end of February, now the middle of March?

[184:04]Well, the timeline was sort of interesting. The whole idea for this conflict began, or I could say

[184:10]that the US began to mobilize for the conflict back in January. And the circumstance in January

[184:16]is that there actually was, or at least there appeared to be, an organic uprising in Iran.

[184:23]There were, according to estimates from Western media, which might not be reliable.

[184:28]There were hundreds of thousands of protesters across the entire country,

[184:32]in every major city across the whole breadth of Iran. And these protests swelled in size. They

[184:39]went on for weeks. And then the Iranian regime cracked down on them, according again, to Western

[184:45]media in an indiscriminate and an extremely violent way. And Washington and Israel were under

[184:51]the impression that that violent crackdown might actually fuel the dissent, might actually fuel

[185:01]the opposition to the government. And so when the US and Israel first considered strikes,

[185:07]it was in a circumstance where you actually had perhaps a latent anti-government ground force.

[185:15]That perhaps if the US and Israel destroyed Iranian weapons or scattered the IRGC

[185:22]by killing the leadership, destroying regime targets, then maybe the people could actually

[185:28]seize control of government assets and arm themselves or seize control of infrastructure

[185:33]or government buildings. And in a word, maybe there would be an opening for there to be some

[185:38]instability in Iran. And maybe like I said, an anti-government movement actually coalesced

[185:44]and become viable to replace the revolutionary Islamic regime. But that was in January.

[185:52]And the protests appeared to die down almost at the exact same time that we began to ramp up

[185:58]our military mobilization. And by the time the US got all of the assets in place or out of place,

[186:05]by the time we got aircraft carriers and destroyers and half of our air force in the Middle East

[186:12]and most of our personnel out of the bases that were not protected by

[186:17]Patriot missile batteries or THAT systems, the protests had already been crushed

[186:22]and it been crushed actually a month ago. So we went into the conflict really just

[186:29]wishing for the best. It's actually a head scratcher. And I think that when all of this is said

[186:33]and done, the history books will show that this was a catastrophe. This was just like a completely

[186:40]unmitigated disaster. And ultimately an unforced error, a war of aggression, a war of choice

[186:49]that was conducted with no planning hastily without a clear idea of an end game and exit plan.

[186:56]You could even say a plan in the middle like there just seems to be from the beginning

[187:00]until the end, no concrete idea of how this would play out. And so when the US went in, we hit

[187:06]him really hard. And where we are two weeks later is that the regime is not just intact.

[187:12]It is totally survived. And now the people have rallied behind it. And the people that have survived

[187:18]inside the regime are now hardliners. Who is the supreme leader? Well, it's the former supreme

[187:24]leader's son. His wife was killed, his daughter was killed, his father was killed. All of his

[187:30]colleagues were killed. This is supposed to be the compliant new leader of Iran that is going

[187:37]to be more willing to or likely to engage in diplomacy that will make more concessions.

[187:44]This is the new government. This is the new regime post decapitation that is going to normalize

[187:50]ties with Israel. Obviously, it's worse. From the point of view of trying to extract compliance,

[187:58]this new Iranian regime is going to be actually vengeful and even more defiant.

[188:05]Not only that, and this is a second aspect of it. In spite of the fact that we've degraded some

[188:10]of their heaviest weapons systems, we sunk their navy, killed the supreme leader, destroyed

[188:15]according to different estimates, three quarters of their missile stockpile or missile launch

[188:21]platforms. Iran still maintains the ability to strike US allies assets, bases, and more importantly

[188:29]shipping in the state of Hormuz. So the regime is intact. It has survived and it's pissed off.

[188:36]It's defiant. It's angry. It's vengeful. They're not going anywhere. And again, there are 200,000

[188:43]members of the Iranian Revolutionary Guard. So if you think we killed 12 of them and now it's a

[188:49]chicken with its head cut off, that's not the case. They're always going to have more people

[188:53]that they can promote. And that's because they prepared for this. They knew what this war would

[188:59]be like. They knew how Israel and the United States would strike. They knew our game plan.

[189:05]And so that's why in the build up to the conflict, they had succession in place, not just the second

[189:13]man or the third man, but down to like the seventh man, the eighth man. In other words, the United

[189:18]States can kill a lot of the leadership before they exhaust the bench in the IRGC clearly as has

[189:25]been demonstrated by the government of Iran. There is no splintering. There is no descent. There

[189:32]is no factionalism. The Iranian Revolutionary Guard and Iranian government has rallied behind a

[189:37]new Supreme leader. They have rallied against the incursion from the US and Israel. And all we

[189:44]manage to do is piss them off. And the second aspect of it again is if Iran is able and maintains

[189:50]a capability to launch missiles, and that's all they need to do. They don't need frigates. They don't

[189:57]need a Navy. They don't even need an Air Force. They don't need any of that. All they need are

[190:03]these very crude and cheap and dumb ballistic missile launch platforms and ballistic missiles.

[190:11]And that is all that they need because Iran is a big country. It's a big country with mountainous

[190:18]terrain, with a massive population. And the United States, they've bombed with Israel 3000 targets.

[190:24]They could bomb 10,000 targets. They could bomb 20,000 targets. Will they have destroyed every

[190:31]missile launch platform? Will they have crippled Iran's ability to make missiles, to make new ones

[190:39]and replace the ones that have been destroyed? The answer is no. They can always source the

[190:45]components because they're cheap. They can always move the missiles around because they're very

[190:49]mobile. They could take them apart and build them back up in another place. And they're always

[190:54]going to have a deep bench of soldiers that are willing to do this. And the more that they can

[190:59]hit, probably the bigger the bench grows. And this is what is happening this week. In spite of

[191:06]everything that has been thrown at Iran just yesterday, there were three ships that were hit

[191:11]in the Persian Gulf, three ships. And many of them, not even in the actual straight. There was one

[191:17]that was hit in the straight of Hormuz two tankers were actually hit closer to Iraq, closer to the

[191:24]coastline of Iraq and Kuwait. So we are two weeks into the war entering the third week. And you

[191:30]still have the threat of missiles being launched straight of Hormuz is like 40 miles long. It's

[191:36]not big. So you actually don't even need a ton of missiles. You only need three. You only need to

[191:43]hit one ship for all the hundreds of ships that are in the Persian Gulf to know that it's not

[191:49]worth the risk to leave the Gulf. That if they try it, they might get hit. There's not a lot of room

[191:55]there. And the threat is still so bad. The US Navy won't even provide an escort. There's been

[192:02]talk in the past week and a half that maybe the US Navy will intervene and you'll have a naval ship

[192:09]as scored a chemical tank or a cargo ship one by one through the straight. I have something in

[192:18]my teeth. But they won't even do that. Even the US warships are not willing to go through the

[192:26]straight of Hormuz. It's too narrow. It's too risky. Iran is still launching surface to see

[192:32]missiles. So even after all this talk and there was talk about the government will provide insurance

[192:38]for the ships. There was talk about the US Navy will provide an escort for the ships. Trump is

[192:44]begging these companies to keep the traffic flowing. Even in spite of this, the US warships won't

[192:50]even go there. Forget about an oil tank or forget about a cargo ship. It's totally closed down.

[192:56]What effect is this having now on the global economy? Well, not only do you have the energy supply

[193:04]constrained because of the shipping choke point. So that's 20% of the world's oil that moves by

[193:10]sea goes through the straight of Hormuz. So it's a huge energy crunch on the global oil market.

[193:16]But then you also have the effect that this is a pipeline. This is a metaphorical pipeline.

[193:23]So you actually have a flow of oil. It's continuous, which means the oil is extracted. The oil's

[193:30]refined. It's put on a ship. It's sent through the Gulf, through the straight to its destination where

[193:36]it is then consumed. Well, it's not just that the oil isn't getting where it needs to go,

[193:42]but it's backed up. The flow of the oil has been backed up, which means that now Kuwait and

[193:49]Qatar and the Emirates and Saudi Arabia, they have to fill up all of their reservoirs of oil.

[193:56]All their storage capacity for oil is reaching 100%, which means they don't have anywhere for

[194:03]all this oil to go. There's another problem here. So not only is the oil not getting towards

[194:08]intended, but where the oil is being made, now the oil's got nowhere to go. So now all of the

[194:15]largest oil producers in the world in the Middle East are among the largest oil producers in the

[194:21]world. They're cutting back on production as well. This is a very costly, this is extremely

[194:28]costly all the way around to not have the oil go through. Having hundreds of tankers stuck,

[194:35]it's going to be very costly to eventually get them out. It's the cost of storing the oil and

[194:42]then it's the cost of reducing production and then ramping it back up. And all of this is creating

[194:48]inflation. All of this is creating very severe and acute economic problems for a global economy,

[194:55]which is already fragile. For example, in the United States, what has been the economic game in the

[195:01]United States for the past few years? It is this tricky calibration. How do we increase economic

[195:10]growth without increasing prices? It has to do with interest rates. We have had chronically and

[195:17]persistently high inflation for a number of reasons. Prices are rising, prices are getting out of

[195:23]control as everybody knows. At the same time, job market is cooling off. Businesses are not hiring

[195:31]more people. Those that have been fired are having a very hard time finding new work. I think the

[195:37]median time is like eight weeks or something people are out of a job for a very long time.

[195:42]Economic growth might be slowing down, spending might be slowing down. There's some worrying trends

[195:47]there, some recession indicators. And so the question facing the Trump White House and the federal

[195:53]reserve is if we increase interest rates or rather keep them high, well, then you're going to

[196:01]tamp inflation down and maybe prices can get back under control and that will alleviate some of

[196:07]the economic pain for the middle class, working class, people living paycheck to paycheck.

[196:13]But if you keep interest rates too high, then businesses might not want to invest and then the

[196:19]economy can grow. And so you have a low growth economy, even if prices stabilize, then people are

[196:25]still not going to get higher salaries. Maybe there's going to be increased unemployment.

[196:30]Jobs are not being created. So you have low growth, which is very bad if you have rising debt

[196:36]because in order to keep up with the pace of the rising debt and deficits, you need to grow your

[196:40]way out of it. Conversely, we're cutting interest rates. Cutting interest rates stimulates

[196:47]investment. People are going to take their money out of the bank, investors, banks,

[196:52]businesses, funds, they're going to take their money out of the bank and they're going to invest

[196:56]it in the economy. And you're going to grow the economy. You're going to get more jobs. You're

[196:59]going to have more business creation. Maybe you're going to be able to grow a little bit more and

[197:03]get out of the shadow of this mountain of debt. But if you cut interest rates, then you also might

[197:09]have an increase in prices. The velocity of money increases are going to be more spending.

[197:14]And if there's more spending, there's more demand that's going to drive the prices up.

[197:18]This is a very delicate equation. This is what got Joe Biden out of office and ultimately

[197:23]Kamala Harris to lose the election. This has obviously been a sore spot for the president as well.

[197:30]He's not happy with the fact that prices have not completely stabilized or then come down.

[197:36]He's frustrated with the Federal Reserve Chairman who is reluctant to cut interest rates

[197:41]and in Trump's opinion, then unleash economic growth. So it's in the middle of this very delicate

[197:47]game that we're playing where we might actually ultimately wind up with both low growth

[197:52]and high prices that now you have an energy shock. Energy is beneath everything else.

[197:59]Shipping, transportation, industry, it's all powered by fossil fuels. It's all powered by energy.

[198:08]Everything moves on freight. Everything moves on ships. Everything flies and planes. Industry,

[198:14]households need energy for their electricity for machines. You have a global increase in energy

[198:22]cost. You have an increase in everything cost. You haven't increased in all prices.

[198:27]And by the way, I don't need to remind you it's a midterm election year.

[198:32]So think about what we got ourselves into. This is the mind boggling nature of the conflict.

[198:39]We start a war with Iran. Think about these questions that were just maybe never considered or

[198:46]never got a sufficient answer before we started dropping the bombs. We're planning on going to

[198:51]war with Iran. We're looking for a regime change. Now to get a regime change, you need a ground

[198:58]force. And we know that we know that you can't get a regime change without dropping or rather

[199:04]just by dropping bombs. We go into it determined that we're going to once and for all confront Iran,

[199:11]it will be decisive, meaning that the regime will be defeated and ousted. That's the stated goal.

[199:18]That's the intention. That's actually the strategic imperative. We go into the war thinking

[199:24]we're going to topple the regime. And the war doesn't work if that doesn't happen.

[199:31]Well, we can't topple the regime without a ground force with air power alone. But we go into it

[199:36]a month after the protests have ended. We start thinking this is a good idea because there's massive

[199:42]protest. Those will be the ground force. If we hurt the Iranian military, well, the people might

[199:48]take their guns or take their assets or overpower them and then you get that's your ground force.

[199:53]That's your regime change. So we need a ground force, but we don't have one. We need to win the war

[200:01]quickly. And that means toppling the regime. But we can't do that. We know that the first thing Iran

[200:09]is going to do if we hit them is close the straight of Hormuz. We have no plan to reopen it.

[200:15]We have no ability to reopen it. We're in a midterm election year. In order to win the midterms

[200:21]or to be competitive, you need to have a stable economy. You need to be popular. Well, this is not

[200:26]only the most unpopular war in history, but it's also raising all prices. It's also actually going

[200:31]to cause inflation, closing the straight of Hormuz necessarily means all prices are going to rise.

[200:38]It's going to increase energy prices that will increase the rest of the prices. And that effect

[200:44]will linger probably four years. And so when you think about all the constraints, there's a logic to

[200:53]all of this was none of this considered. Did they not think through any of it? You really have to

[201:00]scratch your head and wonder what exactly was the plan here? What was the goal? Because again,

[201:07]if you need to win a fast war, then you need to topple the regime. If you need to topple the

[201:12]regime, you need a ground force. We knew that none of that would happen. We knew that we weren't going

[201:18]to topple the regime because we knew we couldn't do it without a ground force and we knew we didn't

[201:23]have one. We knew they would close the straight of Hormuz. And if we didn't topple the regime,

[201:29]we knew it would be closed for a prolonged period of time. And if it's closed for a prolonged

[201:34]period of time, there's no way to open it up. You can't destroy every missile in Iran. You can't

[201:40]destroy every mine or minelaying vessel. And if you can, you can't do it more quickly than they can

[201:46]build new ones. And if you can, you can't do it in such a short amount of time that it doesn't have

[201:52]these ramifications. So in other words, this was in a way inevitable. It was inevitable that we

[202:01]would get sucked into a prolonged attritional war that would hurt the economy, that would damage

[202:07]the political prospects of the Republicans. It was inevitable because we knew it wouldn't be

[202:12]fast because we knew we couldn't topple the regime because we knew we didn't have a ground force.

[202:19]So what was the logic here? Now, this is the latest from the New York Times about the crisis

[202:24]in the strait about how Iran appears to be winning. It says, quote, Iran has begun laying

[202:31]mines in the strait of Hormuz, according to US officials, an effort that could further complicate

[202:37]US efforts to restart shipping there. While the US military said it had destroyed larger Iranian

[202:42]naval vessels that could be used to quickly lay mines in the strait, Iran just began using smaller

[202:48]boats for the operation on Thursday. Again, it's like we go in there bombing their navy,

[202:53]bombing the big stuff. This is our weakness. The current capabilities, the current state of the

[203:00]art of warfare actually favors the defensive side because unmanned vessels, unmanned vehicles are

[203:08]cheap and they're easy to make drones are cheap and easy to make missiles are cheap and mobile

[203:15]and easy to make. So you can be as big and sophisticated and technologically superior as possible.

[203:22]If you can't destroy every single one of these very tiny, very cheap, very robust weapons,

[203:29]you're not going to achieve your objectives. That's the problem. So we destroyed all the frigates.

[203:35]Well, they just started using smaller ships that lay mines and we can't destroy all of those.

[203:42]It says strikes have hit multiple vessels in the area, some of which Iran claimed responsibility for

[203:50]on Tuesday and Iranian deputy foreign minister denied that Iran was mining the strait.

[203:55]In his first remarks, since the war broke out, Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Hamine said in

[204:01]a written statement on Thursday that the lever of blocking the strait of Hormuz must continue to be

[204:06]used. The new mining effort is not particularly fast or efficient, but the Iranians appear to be

[204:11]hoping they can lay them faster than the US can clear them and therefore create a further

[204:16]deterrent for ships moving through the strait. The US military said this week that it attacked

[204:23]16 Iranian mine laying ships. So this is the current state of the conflict. And again,

[204:30]it's a head scratcher because we knew it would go this way. How one, we knew all this information

[204:37]going in to it already happened. This is exactly what happened last year in June. Almost exactly.

[204:48]Israel started the war in June of last year without the United States. Unlike this conflict,

[204:56]they launched the war in a surprise attack and they did it with help from inside of Iran. There

[205:01]were missile and drone strikes from inside the country against Iran's leadership. Iran was hit

[205:07]with a cyber attack that disabled its eyes and ears, all of its radar, early warning systems,

[205:12]everything like that. And then Israel came in with the Air Force and they bombed the nuclear

[205:17]infrastructure, regime targets, missile platforms, everything. And they did this for two weeks.

[205:24]But within days of that initial strike, Iran fixed all of its cyber problems, got their

[205:31]Air Force off the ground. And then Iran started launching these huge waves of ballistic missile

[205:37]attacks, hundreds at a time. And it didn't take long for these ballistic missile waves and drones

[205:44]and cruise missiles to completely overwhelm Iran's defenses. And what we saw in last year's war

[205:50]is that eventually these Iranian missiles were just slamming in a televive every single day.

[205:55]And if Iran would shoot down 90% of the missiles or 85% of the missiles, well, if you shoot

[206:02]100 of them, 15 ballistic missiles are going to get through. And they're going to land in these

[206:07]western first world developed cities, killing a lot of people and destroying a lot of infrastructure

[206:13]and causing a lot of problems. And it was during that war that Israel was effectively strangled

[206:19]in the same way as the Persian Gulf. And Israeli refugees were fleeing the Cyprus. It's actually a

[206:24]big problem now and certain Greek islands. And all of the air traffic over Israel was suspended.

[206:31]People couldn't escape the country. And the economy ground to a halt because people were in

[206:36]bunkers for two weeks because they were getting slammed with missiles every single day.

[206:41]And that is ultimately why the United States was dragged into that war. Because only the United

[206:47]States could actually finish the job that Israel started. At that time, the US was not being hit.

[206:54]Our bases, our assets, our other allies were not being struck. It was only Israel.

[206:59]We intervened at the tail end and forced an end to the conflict by bombing Iran.

[207:05]And then thereby compelling them into a ceasefire, into a truce with Israel. So in other words,

[207:14]we already lived this. We saw this. We saw the economics. We saw the attritional aspect of it.

[207:20]The resilience of Iran's personnel and equipment, their ability to build lots of ballistic missiles

[207:27]and launch them without Israel, meaningfully being able to suppress this capability.

[207:33]Now here we are in the second week. And we've hit Iran way harder than we did last year. Way harder.

[207:40]Way more ordinance, more targets, killed more people, destroyed more stuff, destroyed all the

[207:45]big stuff. And yet, just today, Iran bombed the airport in Bahrain, Iran bombed the airport in

[207:53]Kuwait, Iran bombed a bank building in the Emirates. And this is having a huge effect on the oil.

[207:59]They like I said, they bombed three ships and the Persian Gulf. So they're choking off the oil.

[208:04]And it's also deeply affecting the Persian Gulf countries, the Gulf Cooperation Council.

[208:10]All these other economies are built on investment tourism, high net worth individuals,

[208:15]their capital markets, their specialized banking services for developing nations.

[208:21]And many people are now reporting on this, that all of these oil rich Gulf countries,

[208:28]they were trying to effectively rebrand as modern hubs. We used to think of the Middle East as a

[208:35]barbaric place where this population pyramid of exploding birth rates, very young Muslims

[208:44]were becoming extremely radical and causing terrorism and they're very low IQ and so it's

[208:49]creating political instability. That is what we thought about the Gulf maybe 25 years ago.

[208:54]In the intervening decades, the Gulf has rebranded think Dubai, think Qatar, think Formula One and

[209:02]WWE in Saudi Arabia. They have rebranded as an entertainment tourism banking finance hub.

[209:10]Well, that doesn't really work if you're getting hit with ballistic missiles at a resort. That

[209:16]doesn't, it doesn't help your image. If you've got billionaire businessmen and you've got

[209:22]extremely niche banking services and these buildings and airports are literally being bombed,

[209:28]they're being bombed at every day by suicide drones and medium and short range ballistic missiles.

[209:34]It doesn't work. So in other words, Iran has forced us into an unwinnable situation.

[209:43]We can't do regime change. It's impossible unless we escalate,

[209:49]unless we engage in a ground war. Otherwise, it's off the table. And if we can't do regime change,

[209:56]we can't stop the missiles because we can't bomb them into regime change. We can't bomb all the

[210:02]missiles. So the only way to stop the missiles is to kill the people that are firing them and get

[210:07]new people that don't want to launch them. That's off the table. So they say, well, we'll bomb all

[210:12]the missiles. You can't, there's too many of them. The country's too big. They're zerg rushing us.

[210:19]It's impossible. You can't destroy all of it. You destroy some of it. They build more of it.

[210:24]You bomb one target. They've already moved the missile somewhere else. You kill one guy.

[210:31]There's another guy ready to take his place and he's a grier. He's pissed off because he liked

[210:35]the other guy. He liked the first guy. And in the meantime, as the days drag on, the economic situation,

[210:44]it compounds. It gets worse all the time. If we can't topple the regime, if we can't stop the

[210:50]missiles, then that means the straightest close, the energy doesn't flow. The energy doesn't flow.

[210:56]It strangles a global energy market. It increases prices everywhere. It causes panic.

[211:03]And there's a contagion effect. It affects everybody. There's animal spirits involved in this too.

[211:09]Panic sets in. That's why when the Trump administration releases oil from the strategic reserve,

[211:15]it makes the situation worse. You might say, how does increasing the supply of oil make the price

[211:22]of oil higher? Trump unloaded 270 million barrels of oil from the strategic reserves. You would think

[211:32]that would ameliorate the supply shock because we've replaced the supply. So the price should stabilize.

[211:38]It actually causes the opposite effect because Wall Street is watching. And Japan is watching.

[211:45]And Europe is watching. And what they see is that we have unloaded all the supplies because

[211:51]it's a crisis. And there's no end in sight. And the pain is acute and is ongoing. So if we release it,

[211:59]we're communicating, we have a big problem. So people are going to bet that oil will remain high

[212:04]for the foreseeable future. It drives up the price actually in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait,

[212:10]they're cutting production. Their storage is full. So they literally have to shut down the refineries.

[212:17]They have to shut down the oil industry because the oil has nowhere to go. They could start pumping

[212:22]into these new pipelines they built in the Emirates and the East West pipeline and pipeline in Saudi Arabia.

[212:29]But who's to say Iran doesn't bomb that too? So maybe they're worried about using it. They've never

[212:34]used it to its full capacity. And then what's more, all these countries, in addition to the oil

[212:41]crunch and the revenue they're not getting, all these countries are also being strangled because

[212:46]nobody wants to live there right now. Nobody wants to visit there. Nobody's going to invest in their

[212:52]capital markets in the long term because they don't want to have to make a stop over into a city

[212:57]that's being bombed by Iran every single day. So we are in an unwinnable situation.

[213:05]It gets worse all the time and in particular, it gets bad for Trump because Trump needs to win

[213:10]the election. And so you have this global energy crisis. It's going to cause a global economic

[213:16]crisis as time drags on. It gets worse and as time drags on, we get closer to the midterm elections.

[213:22]So what does that mean? It means two things. You're either going to one, get an escalation.

[213:30]What's the solution? Well, if we can't stop them because we can't bomb them into regime change,

[213:35]well, what's the obvious next step? What's the obvious escalatory step? Well, we could always do

[213:42]other things that will facilitate a regime change. We could always invade. And so that's option one,

[213:48]is if we're not winning the war, if the bombs aren't working and if the pain is that bad,

[213:54]well, then we'll send in the armed forces and we'll invade and we'll force the regime to come down.

[214:00]I think you know why that's not politically tenable, but that's something that Trump has said. He's

[214:05]considering Pete Hegseth said the same thing. They could always go in that direction. They could go

[214:10]up. The other direction is you back off and you leave. Well, how do you do that?

[214:16]If you create a truce or a ceasefire and you tell the Iranians, we're going to stop bombing you,

[214:22]will you please stop bombing us? Maybe you bring China in on this. China needs do oil. China is being

[214:29]heard by the energy crunch. They want to stabilize the Middle East. And China is maybe Iran's

[214:35]most important partner. So maybe the US and China get together at the trade talks in Europe this

[214:39]week. And we tell China that we'll get Israel to back off. If China gets Iran to back off and

[214:47]we could call the quits, we could say it's a truce. We could say it's a ceasefire. Iran will stop

[214:52]bombing the Persian Gulf. They'll stop bombing the Gulf, the GCC countries and we'll get Israel

[214:59]to stop bombing Iran. The only problem with the de-escalation approach is that how do we do this

[215:08]without losing face? Because in the event that we de-escalate, we need a narrative. Because what we

[215:15]said, again, from the outset is we want regime change. And that's what we want. And that's what we're

[215:20]going to get because we run the show and we're the number one power. Well, what happens after two

[215:25]weeks, we threw everything at them and they're still standing. Well, now we look like a paper tiger.

[215:33]That message reverberates all around the world. And the message is the US is not as powerful

[215:38]as it thinks it is. Its deterrent credibility is undermined. We made a threat. Iran did not back

[215:47]down and we couldn't back it up. We signed a check that we couldn't cash. And so now every other

[215:54]country will realize we should adopt the Iran model. We should have a native drone industry. We

[216:01]should buy ballistic missiles. We should buy Iranian drones. Everybody's watching very closely

[216:06]and they're taking notes about this. Iran too. Iran itself. They become actually the strategic

[216:12]victor because they thwarted the US objectives. Even though they are absolutely weakened, even though

[216:19]they are significantly diminished and vulnerable, in a way, it's a strategic victory because they

[216:26]have reestablished deterrence. The messages communicated that you tried. You tried it. You tried

[216:32]to knock us off and you failed and it backfired. We defended ourselves. We're still here. You

[216:38]went in for the kill shot and you missed the Supreme Leader is dead. Well, we'll call him a martyr

[216:44]and we'll appoint a son to pursue vengeance. You bombed our missile launch platforms. Well,

[216:50]we bombed your precious oil and we control the energy trade. We vetoed the global economy.

[216:56]That's what they'll say. We let oil flow to China through the straight on molested,

[217:03]but we refused your oil. We control the global oil market. We control the global economy. You

[217:10]fuck with us. We put our finger on the button and we shut the whole thing down. You'll think twice

[217:15]next time. That's the message and it rallies the people to the regime. So if there was any idea in

[217:22]the Western imagination that the Iranian people had this aspiration for liberal democracy and

[217:29]feminism and only fans and they want to be free of Islamic theocracy or they don't want sovereignty.

[217:37]Well, what effect does this have? If the United States is absolutely bombing the shit out of your

[217:42]country and your government valiantly and bravely defends your people, well, that increase is

[217:50]support for that government. The US is dropping thousands of bombs on Iran, literally causing a

[217:58]category seven earthquake, a seven point oh magnitude earthquake in Tehran may be dropping

[218:06]bunker buster bombs on their capital city. Thousands of Iranians are dead. Hundreds of

[218:12]schoolchildren were killed in the first strike. You killed their leader. And if Iran,

[218:18]defiantly appoints that guy son and says he was a martyr, we're pursuing revenge. We're going

[218:24]to defend our nation. And if they succeed, what does that do other than in dear people to the regime?

[218:31]What does that do other than actually create a rallying effect and the population coalesces

[218:39]around the regime? Probably this is the greatest IRGC recruiting ad ever. Whereas Hamas is destroyed,

[218:47]Hezbollah is destroyed. Jordan's a bitch. The Saudis are a bitch. All these countries are a

[218:54]bitch to Israel. I'll just throw in Qatar and the Emirates. You don't think I'm paid off.

[218:58]The Emirates in Qatar are bitches. They're defenseless. Iran stood alone. That's leadership.

[219:06]That's leadership for its neighbors. That's leadership for its people.
